title: What is a pointer
instruction:
Imagine a storage unit. You put your stuff inside. A pointer is not the stuff — it is the unit number written on a Post-it note. Give someone the note and they can find your stuff.

In Go:
- `&x` gives you the address of `x` (the Post-it note)
- `*p` reads the value at the address (looks inside the unit)

Run this. Notice that printing `p` gives a memory address like `0xc0000b4008`. Printing `*p` gives the actual value.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	x := 42
	p := &x
	fmt.Println("Value:", x)
	fmt.Println("Address:", p)
	fmt.Println("Value via pointer:", *p)
}
successMessage: The address will look different on every run — memory locations change. But *p always gives 42. The pointer remembers where to find it.

title: Declare a pointer
instruction:
The type of a pointer to an `int` is `*int`. A pointer to a `string` is `*string`.

```
var p *int   // p is a pointer to an int
p = &num     // p now holds the address of num
```

Declare a variable `score` with value `100`. Declare a pointer `sp` that points to it. Print both the address and the value via the pointer.

codeChecks.message: Declare: score := 100
Declare the pointer: sp := &score
Print the value via pointer: fmt.Println(*sp)
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: sp holds an address, *sp holds the value at that address. Two different things — same data.

title: Change a value through a pointer
instruction:
This is where pointers become useful.

If you have a pointer `p` to a variable, you can change the variable's value by writing to `*p`:

`*p = 99` — sets the value at that address to 99.

Run this and notice that changing `*p` also changes `x` — because they point to the same place.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	x := 10
	p := &x

	fmt.Println("Before:", x)
	*p = 99
	fmt.Println("After:", x)
}
successMessage: One change through the pointer, and x changed too. They are not two copies — they are the same memory location seen from two angles.

title: The nil pointer
instruction:
A pointer that has not been assigned an address has the value `nil`.

Using a nil pointer crashes your program — you are trying to read from address zero, which is invalid.

Run this. It checks for nil before using the pointer. Add a line that assigns `p = &age` and prints `*p` after the check.
expectedOutput: p is nil — not safe to use yet

codeChecks.message: Assign p = &age to make p point to age.
Print *p after assigning the pointer.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Always check for nil before dereferencing an unknown pointer. This is how Go avoids the null pointer crashes that plague other languages.

title: Pointers and functions — the copy problem
instruction:
Here is the problem pointers solve.

This function tries to double a number. But it receives a copy — so the original never changes.

Run it. Notice that `num` is still 5 after the call. The function doubled the copy, not the original.
successMessage: Still 5. The function received a copy of 5, doubled the copy to 10, then threw the copy away. The original num never changed. This is pass-by-value in action.

title: Pointers and functions — the fix
instruction:
Fix the `double` function so it actually modifies the original.

Change the parameter to `*int` and pass `&num` when calling it.

Inside the function, write `*n = *n * 2` to update the value at the address.

codeChecks.message: Change the parameter to *int: func double(n *int)
Update the value: *n = *n * 2
Pass the address: double(&num)
Replace the TODO comments with your fixes.

successMessage: Now it's 10. The function received the address of num, walked to that address, and doubled the value there. The original changed.

title: Pointer to a struct
instruction:
You used pointer receivers in the Structs chapter. Here is what is happening under the hood.

When you have a pointer to a struct, Go lets you use `.` to access fields directly — you do not need to write `(*p).Name` every time. Go figures it out.

Run this. Then update `p.Score` to `95` through the pointer and print it.

codeChecks.message: Update p.Score = 95 through the pointer.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: p.Name and (*p).Name are identical — Go auto-dereferences for you. The dot operator on a pointer is just syntactic sugar.

title: new() — another way to create a pointer
instruction:
`new(T)` allocates memory for a value of type `T`, sets it to its zero value, and returns a pointer to it.

`p := new(int)` — creates an int set to 0, returns a `*int`.

It is less common than `&x`, but useful when you want a pointer without first declaring a variable.

Run this. Then use `new` to create a pointer to a `float64`, set it to `3.14`, and print it.

codeChecks.message: Use new(float64) to create a float64 pointer.
Set the value to 3.14 and print it.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: new() allocates and zeroes. Then you fill it in. Less common than &var but good to know — you will see it in standard library code.

title: When to use a pointer
instruction:
Three rules of thumb:

1. **Mutation** — if a function needs to modify its argument, use a pointer
2. **Large data** — if a struct is big, passing a pointer avoids copying all those bytes
3. **Consistency** — if some methods on a type use pointer receivers, make them all pointer receivers

Run this. Two functions — one receives a copy, one receives a pointer. See the difference.
successMessage: Copy: unchanged. Pointer: changed. Same function logic, different result based on how you pass the data. Now you know when to reach for the pointer.

title: Pointer gotcha — the loop variable trap
instruction:
This is a classic Go bug.

When you take the address of a loop variable, all iterations share the same address — so after the loop, all pointers point to the same (final) value.

Run this and see the bug. Then fix it by adding `v := v` inside the loop before taking its address.
hint: Inside the loop, add: v := v  (this creates a new variable each iteration). Then take &v.

codeChecks.message: Add v := v inside the loop to create a new copy each iteration, then use &v.

successMessage: The v := v line creates a brand new variable each iteration. Now each pointer has its own address. This bug trips up experienced Go developers — you just fixed it.

title: Fix the broken function
instruction:
The `resetScore` function below is supposed to set a student's score back to zero. But it does not work — the score stays at 88 after the call.

Fix it so that calling `resetScore` actually changes the original struct.
hint: Change the parameter to *Student and update the function body: s.Score = 0. Then pass &student in main.

codeChecks.message: Change the parameter to *Student: func resetScore(s *Student)
Pass the address: resetScore(&student)

successMessage: Score is now 0. You spotted the copy problem and fixed it with a pointer. This skill will save you hours of debugging in real Go projects.

title: Build a bank account
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a bank account:

1. Define an `Account` struct with `Owner` (string) and `Balance` (float64)
2. Write a `Deposit(a *Account, amount float64)` function that adds to the balance
3. Write a `Withdraw(a *Account, amount float64)` function that subtracts from the balance — but only if there are enough funds (print `"Insufficient funds"` if not)
4. In main: create an account with balance 100.00, deposit 50, withdraw 30, try to withdraw 200, print the final balance

Expected output:
```
Balance: 120.00
Insufficient funds
Final balance: 120.00
```
hint: func Deposit(a *Account, amount float64) { a.Balance += amount }  Use a.Balance >= amount check in Withdraw.

codeChecks.message: Define an Account struct.
Add a Balance float64 field to Account.
Define a Deposit function.
Define a Withdraw function.
Use a pointer receiver *Account so changes affect the original.
Print "Insufficient funds" when balance is too low.
Format the balance to 2 decimal places with %.2f.

successMessage: A real bank account built with pointers. Deposit and Withdraw modify the original balance because they receive the address — not a copy. You now understand pointers.
